claim: The Currier A/B scribal split encodes the same underlying language but with two distinct homophonic substitution tables of different sizes: Currier A uses a smaller homophone set (lower token count, 11,022 words) with higher per-glyph entropy, while Currier B uses a larger homophone set producing lower per-glyph entropy, such that the weighted average glyph entropy of A-folios exceeds that of B-folios by at least 0.05 bits.
evidence_for: Currier B is more than twice as large as A (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with a richer encoding alphabet
Overall glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is a blend; section-level entropy variation (3.71â€“3.90) is consistent with variable homophone table sizes
High-probability belief [0.677] already supports same underlying language for A and B
evidence_against: Entropy difference between sections may reflect content type (label-heavy zodiac vs. prose recipes) rather than table size
A larger homophone set in B would predict lower hapax ratio in B-sections if homophones map to fewer roots â€” needs verification
No direct folio-level entropy breakdown for A vs B has been provided
test: Partition the corpus into Currier-A and Currier-B folios; compute per-folio glyph entropy for each partition; run a two-sample t-test on the per-folio entropy distributions of A vs. B.
test_metric: Mean glyph entropy of Currier-A folios minus mean glyph entropy of Currier-B folios
test_threshold: Confirm if A-entropy exceeds B-entropy by â‰¥0.05 bits with p<0.05; deny if difference is <0.02 bits or B-entropy is higher.

reasoning: Detecting a systematic entropy asymmetry between scribal hands would constrain whether the A/B split is a language boundary or a cipher-table boundary, eliminating one of the two leading explanations.
